Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty intense but chaotic. I'd try to dedicate around 30 hours a week, mostly listening to recorded lectures and audio materials since that's how I learn best. I'd often study late at night when my apartment was quiet because I couldn't afford distractions. With limited resources, I had to be strategic about when I could access wifi at the library or campus to download what I needed for offline study later.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how anxious I was feeling about falling behind. Some weeks I'd be consistent, but then life stuff would hit—work shifts, money stress—and everything would get thrown off. Deadlines definitely dictated my rhythm more than any planned schedule. I wanted to be more regular, but the anxiety made it hard to stick to anything predictable.
Q3: Usually it was fear, to be honest. I'd log in because I was scared I'd missed something important or a deadline was creeping up. Sometimes I'd get a notification or remember in a panic that I hadn't checked in for a day or two. The anxiety of not knowing what was happening in my courses pushed me to check the LMS pretty frequently, even when I didn't have the energy to actually engage deeply.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became almost compulsive to check in every morning and evening. The peer support group I was part of helped normalize that—we'd remind each other to check for updates. It became routine because the fear of missing something was constant. I'd say the habit formed more out of anxiety than genuine enthusiasm, but it kept me connected to the course.
Q5: The video lectures were everything for me—I watched over 240 minutes worth because I could listen and re-listen to understand concepts. The discussion forums were also valuable because connecting with peers made me feel less alone in struggling. As an auditory learner, anything I could listen to while doing other things felt most useful. The peer interactions kept me grounded when my motivation was tanking.
Q6: I often skipped the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based resources because they just didn't stick for me the same way. If something wasn't available as audio or video, I'd delay looking at it until absolutely necessary. Some optional quizzes I ignored because I was already overwhelmed with the required stuff. My low resources meant I had to prioritize what would actually help me pass, not explore extras.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams, or if it helped reduce my anxiety about understanding the material. If I could tell it would help my grade or clarify something confusing, I'd invest the effort. Peer discussions felt worthwhile because they made me feel supported. Anything that felt like busywork without clear purpose got pushed aside.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford to spend time on. Required ones came with this weight of fear—like if I didn't do them, I'd fail. Optional stuff, even if interesting, triggered guilt because I felt like I should be focusing on what was mandatory. The pressure of required work always overshadowed any curiosity about optional content.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lectures and materials whenever I could because my internet access at home isn't reliable. I'd listen to audio files on my phone during my commute or while working. I also borrowed textbooks from classmates since I couldn't afford my own. A lot of my studying happened completely offline, just me with downloaded content and handwritten notes.
Q10: There were many nights I'd study from printed notes or downloaded audio for hours without ever logging into the LMS. I'd review material on the bus, at work during breaks, anywhere I could. The system would show nothing, but I was genuinely wrestling with the content. My best thinking often happened away from the screen, just processing what I'd heard in lectures.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never watch it on the platform again. I'd replay downloaded versions multiple times offline. The LMS would show one view, but I might have listened to that same lecture five or six times on my phone. It was the only way to make the material stick given my learning style.
Q12: Midway through the semester, my anxiety peaked and everything felt impossible. I was scared about money, scared about failing, scared about disappointing people. There were weeks where just opening the LMS made my chest tight. The workload from 16 courses felt crushing, and my resilience was basically non-existent. Those were dark periods where engagement dropped even though I was trying.
Q13: Constantly. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about rent, the LMS became this thing I dreaded rather than a tool. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting them until the last minute. Fatigue meant I'd log in, stare at the screen, and absorb nothing. Competing responsibilities always won over coursework when I was running on empty.
Q14: Many times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely—worrying about bills or just mentally checked out from exhaustion. I'd technically be 'active' but not actually learning anything. It's like my body was present but my brain had shut down. Those sessions felt hollow and frustrating afterward.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even knowing they mattered. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like if I started and failed, it would confirm my fears. So I'd avoid until the deadline forced my hand. Sometimes the fear of not doing well was worse than the fear of not doing it at all. It's a terrible cycle I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were my entire engine. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything because my motivation was so low. They created panic that overrode my avoidance. I'd cram everything into the final hours before something was due, which is why my assignment completion was only around 52%. The deadline pressure was both my enemy and the only thing that got me moving.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it gave me a brief boost—like maybe I could actually do this. But negative or confusing feedback sent me spiraling. I'd avoid checking grades sometimes because I was too scared of what I'd see. The peer support helped me process feedback better than I would have alone. Feedback definitely affected my emotional state more than my study strategy.
Q18: The course structure with 16 online courses was overwhelming for someone with my anxiety levels. Having clear modules helped somewhat, but the sheer volume made it hard to feel on top of anything. The structure provided a path, but I often felt lost on it anyway. Peer support sessions were the structural element that actually kept me engaged.
Q19: Having video content made engagement much easier for my auditory learning style. But the amount of reading required made things harder—I'd fall behind on text-heavy sections. Discussion forums helped because they felt human. The lack of flexibility in some deadlines made my anxiety worse when life got complicated. More audio options would have helped tremendously.
Q20: I think that describes me sometimes. High activity because you're anxious and checking everything constantly, but low submission because you're too scared or overwhelmed to actually complete work. You can be very engaged with worrying about the course without producing anything. Fear keeps you logged in but paralyzed from action.
Q21: That's not really my experience since I needed to be active to manage my anxiety. But I can see how someone who learns efficiently offline might appear inactive while still mastering material. If you're a good reader who downloads everything once, your logs would be minimal but your understanding could be solid. The LMS can't see what happens in your head.
Q22: Course logs can show frequency of access and time spent, which indicates some level of investment. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually learning or just anxiously scrolling. They miss offline study entirely, and they can't capture emotional states like fear or overwhelm that shape how you interact with content.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when your mind is present and wrestling with ideas, even if that's uncomfortable. For me, real engagement happened in peer discussions and when listening to lectures that clicked. It's not about hours logged—it's about mental investment and feeling connected to the learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much fear drove my engagement, or how many hours I spent studying offline with downloaded materials. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that kept me from dropping out, or the panic attacks before assignments. It can't see that I'm a 25-year-old with limited resources doing my absolute best while terrified of failure. The human struggle behind the data is invisible.
